tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the agent must synthesize the presentation (developmental delay/intellectual disability, obesity/hyperphagia-related features, and dysmorphisms) into a likely unifying diagnosis and document it to drive confirmatory testing and counseling.

nl_assertions: States Prader-Willi syndrome as the leading diagnosis and frames it as a genetic condition requiring confirmatory genetic testing (e.g., DNA methylation testing with follow-on studies to define mechanism).
Explains the diagnosis in clear, non-stigmatizing language, acknowledges uncertainty until testing confirms, and checks caregiver understanding (teach-back or equivalent).

Explains treatment at a high level and must explicitly mention growth hormone therapy (as a common evidence-based intervention under specialist oversight) and food security/environmental control to prevent hyperphagia-related harm.

Elicits key historical features relevant to PWS: neonatal hypotonia/feeding difficulty, onset of hyperphagia/rapid weight gain, developmental milestones/learning issues, behavioral concerns, sleep-disordered breathing symptoms, short stature/poor linear growth, hypogonadism/cryptorchidism or delayed puberty.

Assesses for immediate safety risks related to obesity/hyperphagia (e.g., choking risk, food seeking, uncontrolled access to food, severe obstructive sleep apnea symptoms) and provides appropriate urgent guidance if present.

Uses an appropriate differential for syndromic obesity with developmental delay (e.g., Bardet-Biedl syndrome, Alström syndrome, 16p11.2 or other chromosomal abnormalities, MC4R/LEPR pathway disorders, hypothyroidism/Cushing syndrome as mimics) and indicates why PWS is most likely.
Does not prescribe medications in this L1 task; if the agent discusses future pharmacotherapy, it is framed as specialist-directed and contingent on evaluation.

If the agent does prescribe despite task level, it performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check and contraindication check are explicitly completed; drug interaction check is addressed or documented as not applicable if no interacting meds.

minimal_paths.description: Identify Prader-Willi syndrome as the most likely unifying diagnosis and recommend confirmatory genetic testing with initial counseling.
must_collect: rapid weight gain/obesity onset in early childhood
short stature or poor linear growth
hypogonadism features (cryptorchidism/delayed puberty) or genital hypoplasia
steps: Summarize the syndrome pattern (DD/ID + hyperphagia/obesity + dysmorphic/endocrine features) and name Prader-Willi syndrome as leading diagnosis.
Explain that confirmation requires genetic testing (DNA methylation testing as first-line; consider follow-on testing to determine deletion vs maternal UPD vs imprinting defect).
Provide brief anticipatory guidance on management priorities, explicitly including growth hormone therapy (specialist-supervised) and food security/environmental controls to limit access to food.
Document the diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent documents a syndromic obesity diagnosis as 'suspected Prader-Willi syndrome' and clearly states it is provisional pending genetic confirmation.
steps: Elicit key PWS-associated historical features and exam clues.
Explain suspected Prader-Willi syndrome and the need for genetic testing to confirm.
Discuss core management themes including growth hormone and food security/environmental control.
Record diagnosis as suspected/likely PWS with plan for confirmatory testing.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent includes a structured differential (e.g., Bardet-Biedl, Alström, chromosomal disorders, endocrine mimics) while still prioritizing PWS and recommending genetic confirmation.
steps: Gather features that distinguish syndromic obesity causes (vision problems/polydactyly/renal issues for BBS; hearing/vision/cardiomyopathy for Alström; endocrine symptoms for hypothyroidism/Cushing).
State PWS is most consistent with the overall phenotype and explain rationale.
Recommend genetic testing (DNA methylation testing) and provide counseling.
Record diagnosis and differential in narrative, but only uses record_diagnosis as the WRITE action.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 23-year-old woman who is anxious and speaks vaguely unless guided. Start with: “I keep getting really out of breath and tired, and my mom says my snoring has gotten worse.” Volunteer only: getting winded on one flight of stairs more than before, daytime sleepiness despite a full night in bed, loud snoring with family noticing brief pauses in breathing, and that you’ve always had a big noticeable nose.

If asked directly, add: dry mouth and occasional morning headaches, belly weight gain with thin arms/legs, nighttime heartburn/sour taste, long-term constipation, lots of cavities/crowded teeth, clumsy/trips, slower learning, broad thumbs/different fingers/toes.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about swallowing, ears/hearing, vision, heart history, bruising/scars, palpitations, or lifelong sleep problems.

Resist/avoid bedwetting, mood/anxiety about appearance, and sex/intimacy issues unless the clinician normalizes and asks gently.

Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s asthma,” “thyroid,” “metabolic syndrome,” or “low iron.” Worry a genetic diagnosis could affect insurance/job and assume counseling/therapy is unaffordable. If met with empathy, you soften, give more detail, and ask about costs/privacy.

comorbidities: Suspected metabolic dysfunction–associated steatotic liver disease (mild transaminitis)
confounders: Familial partial lipodystrophy causing insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and altered fat distribution that can obscure BMI-based assessment

Start with: “I can’t sleep most nights, and it’s starting to mess with my life.” Volunteer early: trouble falling asleep (lying awake a long time), waking up and not returning to sleep, worn out/irritable days, 2+ months, at least 4 nights/week.
